summary_en: The student has weak attendance and minimal exercise completion, requiring urgent intervention. The facilitator attempted to contact the parent without success, and the student's situation remains unchanged.
The student has been repeatedly absent due to late-night gaming. The facilitator contacted the parent multiple times, but the parent stopped responding, and the student's situation has worsened.

The student attended for only 25 minutes and left upset without explanation. She was absent the next day; the facilitator called the parent, who reported a family problem and said the student would return next week.
The student initially struggled but improved after the facilitator provided extra explanation after class. The parent called to express gratitude and reported increased confidence.
The student attends all sessions but does not complete any exercises and does not see their value. The parent was defensive and uncooperative when contacted, making it difficult to help the student.

The student avoids difficult questions and struggles with low self-confidence; after the facilitator discussed confidence-building, the student began showing improvement but still needs psychological support.

The facilitator attempted to contact the student's parents multiple times without success, and the student missed a test. Eventually the father was reached; he was unaware of the program but wants the student to continue. The facilitator notes that earlier communication with parents would have improved the situation.
The student has been leaving sessions early (50 minutes, then 45 minutes) and appears uncomfortable, but denies any specific issues when asked privately. No parent contact has been made.

The student attends sessions but does not practice, resulting in a low test score of 58. The facilitator discussed the importance of practice with the student, but the student believes understanding in class is sufficient. No contact with the parent is documented.
The student (referred to as Amal in the notes) has been crying due to family problems but continues to attend sessions; the facilitator checked in and provided emotional support.
The student has weak attendance and low grades, needing comprehensive support. No parent contact documented.

The student (referred to as Layla in the notes) attended on 2025-10-11 with an excellent test score of 88 but was absent on 2025-10-14. The facilitator sent a WhatsApp message to the parents on 2025-10-14 and has not received a response.
The facilitator discussed exercise quality with the student, who began attempting more difficult questions with slow but steady progress. No parent contact is recorded.
The student is struggling in all areas and requires urgent intervention. No parent contact documented.

The facilitator called the parent twice, both times reaching the mother. The student (referred to as Maryam in the notes) has been attending only partial sessions (30 minutes then 20 minutes) due to tiredness or illness. The mother reports the student insists on attending but is unwell; the facilitator advised rest. Attendance is decreasing.

The notes describe a student (referred to as Hassan) who attends regularly but does not practice, similar to another student named Khalid. The facilitator suggests a broader awareness campaign about the importance of exercises. No parent contact or intervention outcome is recorded.
The student is experiencing anxiety, has poor attendance, and has completed almost no exercises.

The facilitator notes that the student is completing many exercises but only at an easy level, and test scores indicate this is insufficient. As of the latest note, the student has not changed strategy and requires closer follow-up.
The student is identified as struggling and requires an intensive intervention plan.

The student attended all week but suddenly stopped coming despite a good test score of 84. The facilitator attempted to contact the parent via WhatsApp and phone calls but received no answer; a follow-up call is planned for the next day.
The student attends sessions well but completes zero exercises. The facilitator contacted the parent, who was unaware exercises were required; after clarification, improvement is expected.

The student (referred to as Fatima in the notes) appears distracted and checks her phone during class. The father sent a WhatsApp message stating she was sick all week and would return Monday. She returned but seems behind academically; the facilitator offered extra help after class, which the student refused.
The student's performance is poor across all measures; immediate contact with parents is needed.
The student was initially completing many easy exercises without sufficient challenge, but later began attempting difficult questions and showed good progress.
The student is struggling academically with weak grades, attendance, and exercise completion. The notes refer to the student as 'Ahmed', which differs from the given name.
The student has been attending progressively less class time over two days (45 minutes then 30 minutes) and reports feeling tired, possibly due to illness. No parent contact has been attempted.

The student initially crammed 120 questions in one day before a test, scoring 52 and showing frustration. After the facilitator explained that last-minute cramming is ineffective, the student improved and now practices regularly.
The student needs comprehensive intervention as all indicators are negative. No parent contact noted.
The student has excellent attendance but completes zero exercises. The facilitator plans to contact the parents to explain requirements.

The facilitator called the student's mother to address test anxiety, explaining the test was formative not final. The mother relaxed, the student's score improved to 70, attendance and exercise completion increased, and the facilitator noted the intervention succeeded.
The student is identified as one of the most struggling students, requiring an urgent intervention plan.

The student is completing many easy exercises but not progressing to more challenging material; facilitator notes indicate a need to clarify the difference and move to stronger content. No parent contact recorded.
The student's situation is described as difficult, requiring intensive support. No parent contact or interventions are documented.
The student has been attending for only short periods and appears unwell; the facilitator contacted the parent by phone and advised seeking medical care if the condition persists.

The student attends sessions regularly but completes no exercises; the facilitator observes a pattern across multiple students suggesting a methodological issue where students do not understand exercises are essential to the program.

The facilitator called the parent on 2025-10-05 and reported the student was initially struggling but has improved significantly after an early intervention; subsequent notes show the student's scores and engagement increased markedly.
The student is described as having severe anxiety with all indicators showing red flags. The notes appear to reference a different name than the student on file.
The facilitator notes indicate the student avoids difficult questions and needs more time to build self-confidence. No parent contact is recorded.
The student is consistently described as excellent in facilitator notes. No parent contact or interventions recorded.

The facilitator notes indicate the student needs urgent intervention before the situation worsens further. The notes refer to the student as 'Mohammed', which differs from the given name 'Sara Al-Shammari'.

The student initially attended sessions but did not practice; after the facilitator discussed the student's weak test scores, the student began practicing and showed significant improvement, with early intervention succeeding.
The student is noted as one of the weakest students and requires a rescue plan.
The student progressed from completing only easy exercises to attempting difficult questions, showing improvement in test preparation.
The student's performance is very weak, and the facilitator notes that action is needed quickly.

The student's attendance dropped suddenly; she attended only 20 minutes on one day appearing tired, then stopped attending entirely. The facilitator called the parent but received no answer and plans to contact the school administration.
The student attends all sessions but consistently refuses to complete exercises. The facilitator notes that parental intervention is needed.
The student is described as a struggling case requiring special attention.

The parent called expressing concern about the student's test performance; the facilitator reassured them it was for diagnostic purposes. The student subsequently improved dramatically from the 50s to a score of 92, maintained strong momentum, and is described as a remarkable success story.
The student was previously high-performing (score 84) but has suddenly stopped attending. The facilitator attempted to call the parents on 2025-10-14 but received no answer.
The student is noted as struggling and needing comprehensive support.
The student attends sessions but does not practice at all; the same pattern continues over multiple observations.
The student's situation requires rapid intervention according to facilitator notes.
The student initially needed to understand the importance of challenging exercises for growth, and later began challenging himself more with exercises.
The student is described as a clear case of struggling and needs an action plan. The note uses a different name than the student's given name.
The student has perfect attendance but zero exercise participation across multiple sessions. No parent contact recorded.

The student attended only 25 minutes on October 13 and 10 minutes on October 14, reporting a persistent headache. The facilitator called the father, advised a hospital visit, and suggested the student rest the next day.
The student continues to show the same pattern of high volume but easy-level work, prompting a need for strategy change and firmer intervention. No parent contact documented.

The student expressed no benefit from the exercises and only attends because the school requires it. The facilitator called the parent about an attitude issue; the parent was defensive. The student subsequently stopped attending, and the facilitator believes she has quit the program.
The student attends sessions but does not complete any exercises. The facilitator notes a second issue added to the list and indicates a systematic solution is needed.

The student has been consistently arriving 48-52 minutes late to class. The facilitator called the parent, who explained that working night shifts prevents waking the student in the morning; moving to an afternoon session was suggested but no afternoon classes are available, and the lateness continues.
The student attends sessions but consistently refuses to participate in exercises. The facilitator notes a need to understand the reason for the ongoing refusal.

The student attended for only 15 minutes on 2025-10-13 before leaving early due to feeling unwell. The facilitator called the parent, who reported fever and a cold, and advised the student to rest and not return until recovered. The student was absent on 2025-10-14, and the parent indicated the student is still sick but expected to return next week.
The student was absent for a week and the parent was unaware of the program; after intervention, attendance and exercise completion have significantly improved and the parent has been informed.
The student avoids difficult questions and requires ongoing psychological support; the underlying issue appears deeper than academic exercises. No parent contact is documented.
The student attends regularly but completes zero exercises. No parent contact is recorded in the notes.

The student initially had poor attendance and low exercise completion, but after receiving individual explanation after class, showed dramatic improvement scoring 78 on a test. The parent called to express gratitude, noting the student is now confident and enjoys studying, and the student continues to maintain strong attendance and engagement.
The student attends regularly but refuses to practice exercises; the facilitator suggests a workshop on the importance of practice.

The student attended for only 25 minutes on Oct 13, then 15 minutes on Oct 14 before leaving, and messaged via WhatsApp on Oct 14 saying he could not attend. The facilitator called the parent, who said the student was unwell, and advised rest.
The student does exercises but not the type that develops skills and needs guidance. A later note indicates slight improvement in understanding the importance of challenge.
The student attends sessions but refuses to complete exercises, and the situation has worsened to the point where direct intervention is needed. No parent contact is documented.

The facilitator notes indicate the student (referred to as Ali) continues to prioritize quantity over quality in their work, with the facilitator noting a need for clearer guidance. No parent contact recorded.
The student has strong attendance but completes zero exercises, a pattern noted across multiple sessions.

The student's attendance dropped sharply from 90 minutes to 20 minutes, then to 18 minutes, and then the student stopped attending entirely. The facilitator attempted to call the parent (no answer) and sent a WhatsApp message that was not delivered. The facilitator notes the student needs urgent intervention.
The facilitator notes indicate the student is completing only easy-level exercises and needs to progress to harder material; the facilitator plans to provide extra guidance after class.
The facilitator notes describe a student (referred to as Sara) who is not responding to guidance and is part of a pattern observed in several students. No parent contact is recorded.
